$NFLX I just don’t feel like the bullish sentiment has been sufficiently crushed here for them to go the other way with this name just yet. It may not be this ER that we get out of the malaise. There’s just still too much hope for upside from here that people are betting on with options that makes me think the big money is going to keep shorting, keep pressure to the downside. I’m content to wait for the buybacks to do their thing to the share count. Approximately 10% of the float is gone when the buy backs are completed and that has a pretty major impact on the legitimate valuation of this stock. The analysts can poo poo all day long about the competitive environment but that’s always been a thing if you can remember the days of buying premium cable channel subs, this is essentially the exact same situation, with the exception of maybe ads, but even then, we see ads when we watch YouTube, people don’t just stop watching YouTube. I’m going to let the financials do all the talking.

$NFLX The Defection Lie: The article itself admits that Netflix's customer defections are still sitting at industry lows. The subscribers aren't leaving; management is just finding a better way to keep them active between major show releases.  The Ultimate Trojan Horse: By talking about bundling competitors like Peacock as tiles inside Netflix, they aren't retreating—they are becoming the default operating system for TV. They want to be the aggregator that takes a cut from everyone else's content.  Pure Margin Optimization: Setting up live, continuous genre feeds utilizing their existing library costs them next to nothing to maintain, but it creates a non-stop machine for high-margin ad placements.

Citi cuts Netflix’s price target ahead of earnings, but still maintains buy rating  Citi lowered Netflix’s  price target to $100 from $115, implying a 32% increase from Wednesday’s close, while still maintaining its buy rating.  “We believe four factors have put pressure on Netflix’s share price,” analyst Jason Basinet wrote in a Thursday note. “1) tepid viewership, 2) an M&A overhang, 3) perception that Netflix lacks catalysts, and 4) investor enthusiasm for semis, which puts pressure on hyper-scalers and, in turn, pressures Netflix.”  Yet, the analyst remains positive on the streaming giant. Basinet said management has hinted at new pricing tiers in May, serving as a positive catalyst for the stock while helping top-line growth. He also believes that M&A can improve Netflix’s catalogue, improving engagement, viewership, and operating leverage. $NFLX

$NFLX    Everyone is watching the pattern. Few are watching the level that decides the outcome.    Yes, the chart is showing a potential head-and-shoulders setup, but price has not broken the neckline yet.    The interesting part: the previous left shoulder formed a smaller H&S, held support, and eventually turned into the next leg higher.    Could history repeat?    Key levels:    Hold neckline support = bulls keep the structure alive  Lose $68 = bearish confirmation    The chart is at a decision point. Now we find out if this is a breakdown waiting to happen or another shakeout before a move higher.    What’s your read on $NFLX here?
